finalized_plan.description: Parse and summarize the game rules to clearly capture the mechanics of the device, including the three transition scenarios when each piston fires, and identify the key difference between a ball being “ejected” versus “released.”

Formally model the evolution of the platform using a probabilistic framework (e.g., a Markov chain) where the state tracks which balls (by number) occupy positions 1, 2, and 3, and outline the probabilities of transitions based on the three equally likely piston outcomes

Identify and formalize the condition for winning—namely, when the chosen ball is the one that is “ejected” by a piston—and determine which positions (and corresponding transitions) lead to an ejection event

Analyze how the initial ordering (balls 1–100, with 1, 2, 3 on the platform initially) interacts with the transition rules over successive rounds, noting how balls move from the ramp to the platform and how the shifting process may favor certain ball numbers over others

Develop a mathematical or computational simulation (or both, if necessary) to compute the probability that each ball (from 1 to 100) will eventually be in an ejection event rather than just being released, taking into account the repeated rounds and shifting rules

Process the calculations or simulation results to compare the computed winning probabilities for each ball, identifying any systematic bias in the shifting process that confers a higher chance of being ejected on a particular ball

Determine and verify which specific ball number attains the highest overall probability of ejection (and thus winning the prize) by double-checking the derivations/simulation outcomes and running verification tests, if needed
Provide the final answer as the ball number that maximizes your odds of winning the prize
